did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep neural networks (DNNs) for skin cancer classification (dermatology task) using skin lesion images from the ISIC 2018 dataset. The study evaluated the vulnerability of these DNN models to universal adversarial perturbations (UAPs) and investigated adversarial retraining as a defense mechanism.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental results. The authors conducted a study evaluating the vulnerability of deep neural networks to universal adversarial perturbations (UAPs) across three medical image classification tasks (skin cancer, diabetic retinopathy, and pneumonia) using specific datasets (ISIC 2018, OCT, and chest X-ray). The paper includes a Methods section detailing data acquisition and model architectures, a Results section reporting specific success rates and robustness metrics, and a Conclusion based on their findings, which distinguishes it from a review paper.

title: Universal adversarial attacks on deep neural networks for medical image classification
brief_summary: This study evaluated the vulnerability of deep neural networks to universal adversarial perturbations (UAPs) across three medical image classification tasks: skin cancer, diabetic retinopathy, and pneumonia. Using datasets including ISIC 2018 for skin lesions and OCT/chest X-ray images, the authors tested seven model architectures against both nontargeted and targeted UAPs. The results demonstrated that DNNs are highly vulnerable, with almost imperceptible UAPs achieving success rates exceeding 80% for both attack types. The vulnerability was found to be largely independent of the specific model architecture used. Furthermore, the investigation into adversarial retraining as a defense mechanism revealed that it increased robustness in only very few cases, failing to provide comprehensive protection. These findings indicate that DNN-based clinical diagnosis is easier to deceive than previously assumed, highlighting significant security risks in practical medical applications.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories for skin cancer, diabetic retinopathy, and pneumonia
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates three medical image classification tasks: skin cancer classification (7 classes: MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC) using ISIC 2018 dataset, referable diabetic retinopathy classification (4 classes: CNV, DME, DRUSEN, NM) using OCT images, and pneumonia classification (2 classes: NORMAL, PNEUMONIA) using chest X-ray images. The models predict discrete class labels, and performance is measured by accuracy and fooling rates for misclassification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Skin lesion images (red-green-blue color) obtained from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 dataset, specifically from challenge2018.isic-archive.com/task3/training/. It contains 7 classes of skin lesions.
classes_used: benign keratosis (solar lentigo/seborrheic keratosis/lichen planus-like keratosis) (BKL)

use.training: Used for training DNN models for skin cancer classification (7,000 images).
use.testing: Used for testing DNN models for skin cancer classification (3,015 images).

datasets_used.name: OCT Dataset (from Mendeley)
datasets_used.description: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) images of the retina (grayscale) obtained from a previous study [3] hosted on data.mendeley.com/datasets/rscbjbr9sj/3. The original dataset contained 109,309 images, but a class-balanced subset was constructed for this study.
classes_used: choroidal neovascularization with neovascular membrane and associated subretinal fluid (CNV)
diabetic macular edema with retinal-thickening-associated intraretinal fluid (DME)
multiple drusen present in early age-related macular degeneration (DRUSEN)
normal retina with preserved foveal contour and absence of any retinal fluid/edema (NM)

use.training: Used for training DNN models for pneumonia classification.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance of generated UAPs against a baseline of random perturbations to evaluate vulnerability.
To evaluate the vulnerability of DNN models to nontargeted UAPs by calculating the proportion of images misclassified.
To evaluate the vulnerability of DNN models to targeted UAPs by calculating the proportion of images classified into a specific target class.
To analyze the distribution of predicted classes versus true classes for attacked models.
To quantify the magnitude of the perturbations (UAPs) to assess their imperceptibility.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared the success rates of generated Universal Adversarial Perturbations (UAPs) against random UAPs (random controls) using descriptive statistics (mean success rates and standard deviations) and confusion matrices, but did not perform statistical significance testing.

nontargeted_UAP_vulnerability.notes: Values in brackets are Rf of random UAPs (random controls). For p=∞, ζ=4%: Inception V3 (90.0%), VGG16 (86.4%), VGG19 (87.0%), ResNet50 (87.9%), Inception ResNet V2 (90.3%), DenseNet 121 (82.9%), DenseNet 169 (84.2%).

targeted_UAP_vulnerability.metric: Targeted Attack Success Rate (Rs) with p=2, ζ=2%
targeted_UAP_vulnerability.notes: Values in brackets are Rs of random UAPs (random controls). Rs for targeted attacks on MEL was lower overall than Rs of UAPs with p=2. Transferability was weak; UAPs generated from Inception V3 achieved Rf of ~45% on other architectures.

nontargeted_UAP_vulnerability.notes: Values in brackets are Rf of random UAPs (random controls). For p=∞, ζ=6%: Inception V3 (73.9%), VGG16 (74.9%), VGG19 (74.7%), ResNet50 (74.8%), Inception ResNet V2 (74.0%), DenseNet 121 (73.0%), DenseNet 169 (72.3%).

targeted_UAP_vulnerability.metric: Targeted Attack Success Rate (Rs) with p=2, ζ=6%
targeted_UAP_vulnerability.notes: Values in brackets are Rs of random UAPs (random controls). Rs for targeted attacks on NM was lower (30-40%) for VGG and DenseNet models compared to others (~85%). Transferability was weak; UAPs generated from Inception V3 achieved Rf of ~2% on other architectures.

nontargeted_UAP_vulnerability.notes: Values in brackets are Rf of random UAPs (random controls). VGG16 and VGG19 Rf were ~50% because they classified most images into PNEUMONIA and NORMAL respectively. For p=∞, ζ=4%: Inception V3 (79.8%), VGG16 (50.0%), VGG19 (49.3%), ResNet50 (73.0%), Inception ResNet V2 (77.0%), DenseNet 121 (71.7%), DenseNet 169 (71.3%).

targeted_UAP_vulnerability.metric: Targeted Attack Success Rate (Rs) with p=2, ζ=2%
targeted_UAP_vulnerability.notes: Values in brackets are Rs of random UAPs (random controls). Overall high Rs (>85%) observed. Transferability was weak; UAPs generated from Inception V3 achieved Rf of ~10% on other architectures.

Adversarial Retraining Results.notes: Adversarial retraining did not affect test accuracy. It increased robustness (decreased Rf) against nontargeted UAPs in only very few cases. For the OCT dataset, Rf decreased with adversarial retraining iterations. For skin lesions and chest X-ray, weak transferability of UAPs was observed across architectures.

architecture_used.main_model: Seven DNN architectures: Inception V3, VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, Inception ResNet V2, DenseNet 121, and DenseNet 169.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning procedures were performed using pre-trained models (implied by 'transfer learning' and standard practice for these architectures in medical imaging, though specific pretraining datasets like ImageNet are not explicitly named in the provided text, the models are standard Keras applications).
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported in the provided text chunks.

architecture_details.optimizer: Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with momentum of 0.9
architecture_details.learning_rate_schedule: 0.001 for ≤ 40 epochs, 1e-4 for 41–45 epochs, and 1e-5 for > 45 epochs
architecture_details.data_augmentation: Random image rotations (-5° to 5°), random 5% height and width shifts, and oversampling for skin cancer classification to handle class imbalance.
architecture_details.perturbation_norm_parameter: imax set to 15 for UAP generation.

training_details.adversarial_retraining: Evaluated as a defense mechanism. For OCT dataset, Rf decreased with iterations. For skin lesions and chest X-ray, weak transferability was observed. Adversarial retraining did not affect test accuracy but reduced targeted attack success rate (Rs) in some cases (e.g., OCT Rs decreased from ~95% to ~25% after 5 iterations; Chest X-ray Rs decreased from ~95% to ~50%; Skin lesions Rs decreased from ~95% to ~30%).

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: Standard classifier heads associated with the respective architectures (Inception V3, VGG16, VGG19, ResNet50, Inception ResNet V2, DenseNet 121, DenseNet 169) for multi-class classification tasks (7 classes for skin cancer, 4 classes for diabetic retinopathy, 2 classes for pneumonia).
architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not reported in the provided text.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Random image rotations (-5° to 5°), random 5% height and width shifts, and oversampling for skin cancer classification to handle class imbalance.
Dataset construction: Class-balanced subset construction for OCT and chest X-ray datasets by randomly selecting images per class.
Input format: Skin lesion images used as red-green-blue color; OCT and chest X-ray images used as grayscale.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Adopted for skin cancer classification to account for imbalances in the dataset.

conclusions: Deep neural networks (DNNs) used for medical image classification (skin cancer, diabetic retinopathy, and pneumonia) are highly vulnerable to universal adversarial perturbations (UAPs).
Almost imperceptible UAPs achieved success rates exceeding 80% for both nontargeted and targeted attacks across the tested medical imaging tasks.
The vulnerability of DNNs to UAPs was found to be largely independent of the specific model architecture used.
Adversarial retraining, a common defense mechanism, increased robustness against UAPs in only very few cases and did not provide comprehensive protection.
Adversarial retraining did not negatively affect the original test accuracy of the models.
The transferability of UAPs across different model architectures was weak, suggesting that UAPs generated for one architecture may not effectively deceive others.
DNN-based clinical diagnosis is easier to deceive via adversarial attacks than previously assumed, posing significant security risks for practical medical applications.
important_remarks: Deep neural networks are highly vulnerable to universal adversarial perturbations (UAPs), with success rates exceeding 80% for both nontargeted and targeted attacks using almost imperceptible perturbations.
Vulnerability to UAPs is largely independent of the specific model architecture used (tested Inception V3, VGG16/19, ResNet50, Inception ResNet V2, DenseNet 121/169).

Adversarial retraining, a common defense, increased robustness in only very few cases and failed to provide comprehensive protection; for example, ~40% of OCT images remained misclassified after retraining.
Transferability of UAPs across different model architectures was weak, suggesting that UAPs generated for one architecture may not effectively deceive others.
Adversarial retraining did not negatively affect the original test accuracy of the models.
The study highlights significant security risks for practical medical applications, as adversaries can cause failed diagnoses at lower costs without needing to consider specific data distributions.
The OCT and chest X-ray datasets were constructed as class-balanced subsets from a previous study [3] hosted on Mendeley, which may introduce selection bias compared to the original larger datasets.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; results were compared against random UAP controls using descriptive statistics.
